name: Binding Invoker
description: Interface for invoking bindings governed by specific binding specifications across a service boundary. A binding invoker implements one or more binding specifications (e.g., openbindings.openapi@1, openbindings.mcp@1) and knows how to invoke operations through them. This contract is the project's published wire form of binding invocation, for remote invokers that adopt it (delegates, hosted invokers, anything reached over a transport wanting a shared frame shape). In-process implementations (the SDKs' invocation handles) realize the same semantics natively and are not required to expose frames; the frame protocol is the handle, serialized. invokeBinding is a typed bidirectional I/O operation: the caller streams BindingInvokerInputFrame messages in (starting with an `open` frame that carries the BindingInvocationInput, followed by zero or more `input` frames and a terminating `close`), and the service streams BindingInvokerOutputFrame messages back (zero or more `output` frames plus a single terminal `complete` or `error` frame). The same shape covers unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional bindings; cardinality is observed by how the caller drives the frames, not declared in the contract. Authentication and other runtime prerequisites are not carried in the OBI document; instead, when a binding cannot proceed because required context is missing, invokeBinding fails with a CONTEXT_REQUIRED error enumerating what must be satisfied, and the runtime resolves it into context and retries. This keeps prerequisites out of the static interface and uniform across every binding specification.

BindingSpecInfo.description: Information about a supported binding specification.

bindingSpec.description: Binding specification identifier, exact and opaque (e.g., openbindings.openapi@1, openbindings.mcp@1).

description.description: Human-readable description of this binding specification.

Source.description: A binding source (binding-specification identifier plus location or content), as an OpenBindings document carries it. Open to extension fields so a document's source entry forwards verbatim.

bindingSpec.description: Binding specification identifier governing this source.

location.description: Absolute address of the source, per its binding specification (core OBI-D-05).

content.description: Embedded source-artifact representation, per its binding specification.

BindingContext.description: Opaque runtime context for binding invocation, typically supplied by the runtime from its store. Well-known credential fields: 'bearerToken' (string), 'apiKey' (string), 'apiKeys' (object mapping a requirement's 'name' to its key — the scheme-scoped form for the alternative that carries several API keys at once; a scheme looks up its named entry first and falls back to the single 'apiKey' convenience), 'basic' (object with 'username' and 'password'), 'accessToken' / 'refreshToken' / 'expiresAt' for OAuth. Well-known transport fields: 'headers' / 'cookies' / 'environment' (objects with string values) and 'metadata' (object with arbitrary values). Well-known configuration field: 'configuration' (object) — per-invocation configuration-point values, keyed by point name: the operation-invoker's 'selection' point, and the named points each governing binding specification defines for its family (for example, a decode point), with values as each point's definition specifies; entries are consulted at the per-invocation tier of the point's consultation order. Implementations and callers MAY add additional fields for session state, consent flags, or any other invoker-managed runtime data; drivers SHOULD ignore fields they don't recognize.

ContextRequirement.description: One prerequisite that must be satisfied before a binding can be invoked, resolved into context. `type` names the requirement family; the remaining fields are type-specific. The well-known `auth.*` family resolves into BindingContext credential fields: `auth.bearer` produces 'bearerToken', `auth.apiKey` produces 'apiKey' (or a scheme-keyed entry in 'apiKeys' when the requirement carries a `name`), `auth.basic` produces 'basic', and `auth.oauth2` produces 'accessToken' (carrying flow fields such as 'grantType', 'authorizeUrl', 'tokenUrl', and 'scopes'). Other families (e.g. 'approval.user', 'config.value', 'account.link') resolve into their own context fields. A runtime dispatches each requirement to a resolver registered for its `type`.

type.description: Requirement type identifier, e.g. 'auth.bearer', 'auth.oauth2', 'auth.basic', 'auth.apiKey', 'approval.user', 'config.value'. Resolvers register against these; an unrecognized type is unsatisfiable by a runtime that has no resolver for it. An invoker whose source artifact declares a scheme it cannot itself apply still surfaces the requirement with a type derived from the artifact's scheme (e.g. 'auth.http.digest'), so the alternative stays discoverable rather than silently dropped — the surfaced-never-skipped rule each binding specification pins for its own credential carriage.

name.description: The scheme name as the source artifact declares it (e.g. an OpenAPI securitySchemes key). Distinguishes two requirements of the same type within one alternative — two ANDed API keys are otherwise indistinguishable — and keys scheme-scoped credential lookup (see BindingContext's 'apiKeys').

description.description: Human-readable description shown to a user or operator during resolution (e.g. an approval prompt message).

durable.description: Whether resolved context for this requirement MAY be persisted under a storage key the runtime derives from the challenge `target` and reused for later invocations (true; e.g. credentials), or MUST be satisfied freshly for every invocation and MUST NOT be persisted (false; e.g. a one-shot user approval). Defaults to true. A requirement family typically fixes this value.

ContextRequiredDetails.description: The `details` payload of an InvocationError whose `code` is CONTEXT_REQUIRED, and the return shape of prepareBinding. Enumerates the runtime context the invoker needs before it can proceed. `alternatives` are disjunctive: satisfying ANY one suffices. The `requirements` within a single alternative are conjunctive: ALL must be satisfied. This OR-of-AND shape captures real authentication semantics such as 'OAuth2 OR (apiKey AND clientCert)', which a flat list cannot. A challenge is a SCOPE, not a hint: it bounds the context the invoker may receive. A runtime MUST provision only the context that satisfies the one selected alternative (the credentials it names plus non-secret configuration) and MUST NOT hand the invoker other stored credentials; an invoker MUST NOT receive raw context-store access (enumeration or arbitrary reads), only this scoped resolution.

target.description: The target the binding addresses — typically its endpoint URL or host. The runtime uses this to locate and store any context it resolves; how it derives a storage key from the target (for example, normalizing to host[:port] so families sharing a host share context) is the runtime's policy, not part of this contract. The invoker reports the target and neither keys nor stores.

alternatives.description: Disjunctive set of ways to satisfy the requirement. The runtime selects one alternative whose every requirement it can resolve, and provisions only that alternative's context, never the union of all alternatives and never unrelated stored credentials.

requirements.description: Conjunctive set: every requirement in this alternative must be resolved for the alternative to be satisfied.

InvocationError.description: Structured error carried by an `error` output frame. Indicates terminal failure of the invocation. Codes named by a normative rule of either invoker contract — binding-invoker or its operation-invoker peer — (CONTEXT_REQUIRED, ERR_PROTOCOL, ERR_TRANSPORT_CLOSED, ERR_CANCELLED, ERR_VALIDATION_FAILED, ERR_BINDING_NOT_FOUND) are normative where named; other codes are recommended conventions (see the binding-invoker README's registry), and consumers SHOULD handle unknown codes gracefully.

code.description: Machine-readable error code (e.g., ERR_CANCELLED, ERR_VALIDATION_FAILED, ERR_PROTOCOL, CONTEXT_REQUIRED).

details.description: Optional structured details specific to the error code. For code CONTEXT_REQUIRED, `details` is a ContextRequiredDetails object.

category.description: The normative classification axis consumers branch on. One of a fixed, closed set; a consumer that meets an unknown value treats it as `permanent`. `context` is the resolve-and-retry hinge (CONTEXT_REQUIRED); `auth` means supplied credentials were rejected; `cancelled` means the caller ended the invocation; `transient` is a lower-layer condition that may clear on its own; `service` means the target was reached and returned an error of its own; `validation` means a value or the schema graph failed the validation claim; `protocol` means the frame or invocation contract was violated; `permanent` is terminal.

effects.description: Side-effect possibility gating safe retry, present where retry is in question (categories `transient` and `service`). `none`: the call's side effects provably did not take hold — safe to retry. `possible`: the call may have taken effect — not safe to auto-retry a non-idempotent operation. `definite`: the call took effect. Absent means treat as `possible`.

BindingInvocationInput.description: Initial payload carried by the `open` input frame: identifies the binding to invoke and provides context.

BindingInvokerInputFrame.description: One frame from the caller into invokeBinding. The caller streams a sequence: exactly one `open` frame first (carrying the BindingInvocationInput), then zero or more `input` frames carrying values to deliver to the underlying binding, then exactly one `close` frame signaling no more input.

oneOf.title: open
oneOf.description: Open the invocation. MUST be the first frame on the input stream.

oneOf.title: input
oneOf.description: Write one input value to the underlying binding. The value's schema is determined by the underlying binding's operation, not by this contract.

value.description: One input value, opaque at this layer.

oneOf.title: close
oneOf.description: Caller signals that no more input frames will be sent. Idempotent.

BindingInvokerOutputFrame.description: One frame from the service back to the caller. The service streams a sequence: zero or more non-terminal frames (`output`, `input_closed`) followed by exactly one terminal frame (`complete` or `error`).

oneOf.title: output
oneOf.description: One output value from the underlying binding. The value's schema is determined by the underlying binding's operation, not by this contract.

value.description: One output value, opaque at this layer.

oneOf.title: input_closed
oneOf.description: Binding has closed the input side from its end (e.g., a unary binding closes input after the first read). Subsequent caller `input` frames are ignored (never written; see rule 3). The invocation continues; outputs may still flow.

oneOf.title: complete
oneOf.description: Terminal: the underlying binding completed normally. MUST be the last frame.

oneOf.title: error
oneOf.description: Terminal: the underlying binding failed. MUST be the last frame. Carries a structured InvocationError.

openbindings.binding-invoker.listBinding.description: List the binding specifications this invoker supports, by exact identifier.

openbindings.binding-invoker.invokeBindi.description:
Invoke a resolved binding via a typed I/O frame stream. The caller writes a sequence of BindingInvokerInputFrame messages (starting with an `open` frame); the service emits a sequence of BindingInvokerOutputFrame messages ending in either a `complete` or `error` frame. The same shape covers all cardinalities of the underlying binding (unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, bidirectional).

## Normative conformance rules

Service implementations of this operation MUST conform to the following rules. They describe the wire contract, not any particular transport; in-process implementations realize the same semantics through their SDK's native invocation handle, whose behavior these rules mirror.

1. **First frame.** The first input frame received MUST be an `open` frame. If the first frame is any other kind, the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_PROTOCOL` and process no further input.

2. **Single open.** Exactly one `open` frame is permitted per invocation. If a second `open` frame arrives after the first, the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_PROTOCOL`.

3. **Input after input closure (either side).** Once input is closed from either side — the caller has sent its `close` frame, or the implementation has emitted an `input_closed` output frame — any subsequent `input` frame MUST be ignored: it is not written to the underlying binding, the invocation continues, and outputs still flow. The implementation SHOULD surface a diagnostic for ignored frames. Late frames after `input_closed` are an inherent race (the frame may already be in flight when `input_closed` is emitted) and are never a protocol violation; this mirrors the SDK handles, where a write after input closure rejects non-terminally without affecting the invocation.

4. **Exactly one terminal output frame.** The output stream MUST end with exactly one terminal frame (`complete` or `error`). The implementation MUST NOT emit further output frames after a terminal frame. If both completion and failure conditions arise during processing, the implementation chooses one terminal frame (typically `error`, since errors take precedence over completion) and emits only that one.

5. **Transport closure without a terminal frame.** If the underlying transport closes without a terminal output frame having been emitted, the consumer side MUST synthesize a terminal `error` frame with code `ERR_TRANSPORT_CLOSED` before propagating closure to its caller.

6. **Discriminator semantics.** The `discriminator` keyword in the frame schemas is informative under JSON Schema 2020-12; validators that don't recognize it MUST still validate frames correctly by evaluating `oneOf`. Implementations dispatch by matching the `kind` property's `const` value against the variant.

7. **`additionalProperties: false`.** Frame variants declare `additionalProperties: false`. Implementations MUST reject input frames with unknown properties (using `ERR_PROTOCOL`). Output frames SHOULD likewise reject extra properties on the consumer side as a protocol violation.

8. **Missing context (CONTEXT_REQUIRED).** If the binding cannot be invoked because required runtime context is missing (credentials, approvals, configuration, and the like), the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `CONTEXT_REQUIRED` whose `details` is a ContextRequiredDetails object, and it MUST do so before emitting any `output` frame and before causing any observable side effect on the target. This pre-execution guarantee is what makes the runtime's resolve-and-retry safe for non-idempotent operations.

9. **Resolve and retry.** On receiving CONTEXT_REQUIRED, a runtime selects one `alternative` whose every `requirement` it can resolve (alternatives are disjunctive; requirements within one alternative are conjunctive), resolves each requirement into context, provisions to the invoker only the context that satisfies the selected alternative (the named credentials plus non-secret configuration) and no other stored credentials, MAY persist context for requirements whose `durable` is true (the default), under a storage key it derives from `details.target`, for reuse, and MAY retry the invocation with the augmented context. Context for a `durable: false` requirement MUST NOT be persisted or reused and MUST be re-satisfied on each invocation. A runtime SHOULD bound retries; an implementation SHOULD NOT re-challenge for context it was supplied on the current attempt, and SHOULD instead emit a non-CONTEXT_REQUIRED error when supplied context proves insufficient, so the loop terminates. An invoker MUST NOT be given raw context-store access (enumeration or arbitrary reads); it receives only the scoped resolution. When the runtime routes a binding to a separate or third-party invoker (a delegate), it MUST forward only the context that delegate's challenge requires, never the full stored profile.

10. **Requirement types.** `auth.bearer`, `auth.basic`, `auth.apiKey`, and `auth.oauth2` are the first standard requirement family and resolve into the corresponding BindingContext credential fields. Implementations MAY define further families (e.g. `approval.user`, `config.value`). An unrecognized requirement `type` is unsatisfiable by a runtime that lacks a resolver for it; such a requirement simply makes its enclosing alternative unselectable.

11. **Caller cancellation.** When the caller cancels an in-flight invocation (via the SDK handle's `cancel()`, an `AbortSignal`, or a transport-level cancellation signal), the implementation MUST cease emitting `output` frames and emit exactly one terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_CANCELLED` — unless a terminal frame (`complete` or `error`) was already emitted, in which case cancellation is a no-op. This preserves rule 4's exactly-one-terminal guarantee: cancellation races the invocation's own completion, whichever terminal condition is reached first wins, and a cancellation arriving after a terminal frame never overwrites it. In-process handles realize this natively — a `cancel` after the handle has settled is dropped rather than overwriting the settled terminal.

openbindings.binding-invoker.prepareBind.description: Preflight: report the context a binding would require before invocation, without invoking it or causing side effects. Returns a ContextRequiredDetails the runtime can resolve proactively (good for UX, e.g. prompting for auth before a user acts), or null when the invoker cannot determine requirements without invoking (some targets only reveal them via a live CONTEXT_REQUIRED challenge). The operation is always implementable — returning null is the conformant answer whenever requirements cannot be determined statically, so no capability prevents a service from carrying it (correspondence remains per-operation, as for every contract operation). prepareBinding is advisory: the reactive CONTEXT_REQUIRED error from invokeBinding is the authoritative source of requirements. When `context` is supplied on the input, the result reports only the requirements still unsatisfied by that context.
